entries.title: Windows Firewall setting changed
entries.summary: A Windows Defender Firewall profile-level setting was modified.
entries.details: Generated when a Windows Defender Firewall profile setting is changed (e.g., default inbound/outbound action, notifications, unicast responses to multicast/broadcast). Records which profile was changed, the new setting value, and the process or policy source responsible for the change.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

investigationPivots: Identify ProfileChanged to determine which network profile was affected (Domain vs. Private/Public)
Correlate timestamp with process creation events (EID 4688 or Sysmon EID 1) to identify the responsible process
Verify whether the change aligns with a scheduled Group Policy update (EID 4954)
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy pushing standard firewall profile configurations
IT administration tools managing firewall settings centrally
OS installation or major feature updates resetting profile defaults

keyFields.name: ProfileChanged
keyFields.description: The firewall profile that was modified (Public, Private, or Domain)

keyFields.name: NewSettingValue
keyFields.description: The new value applied to the profile setting

keyFields.name: ModifiedBy
keyFields.description: The process or policy source that made the change

entries.title: Firewall rule added
entries.summary: A new inbound or outbound firewall rule was added to the exception list.
entries.details: Generated when a rule is added to the Windows Defender Firewall exception list. Records the rule name, ID, application path, protocol, ports, direction (inbound/outbound), action (allow/block), and the process or policy source that added it.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

investigationPivots: ApplicationPath reveals the binary being allowed — flag paths in user-writable directories (AppData, Temp, Downloads)
Rules with Direction=Inbound and Action=Allow are highest priority for review
ModifiedBy identifies the process or policy source that added the rule
Cross-reference RuleName with known software — generic, randomized, or blank names are suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Software installers adding rules for legitimate applications (e.g., game clients, VoIP, videoconferencing)
Enterprise endpoint management platforms pushing approved firewall policy
Windows components and services adding rules during initial setup or update

keyFields.name: RuleName
keyFields.description: Name of the firewall rule added

keyFields.name: ApplicationPath
keyFields.description: Path of the binary allowed by the rule

keyFields.name: Direction
keyFields.description: Whether the rule applies to inbound or outbound traffic

keyFields.name: Action
keyFields.description: Whether the rule allows or blocks traffic

keyFields.name: ModifiedBy
keyFields.description: Process or policy source that added the rule

entries.title: Firewall rule modified
entries.summary: An existing firewall rule was modified.
entries.details: Generated when an existing Windows Defender Firewall rule is changed. Records the rule name, ID, application path, protocol, ports, and the process or policy source responsible. Adversaries may modify existing legitimate-looking rules rather than creating new ones to avoid detection heuristics that alert only on rule creation.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

investigationPivots: Review preceding EID 2004 for the same RuleId to compare before/after state
Flag modifications that broaden port ranges or remove ApplicationPath restrictions
ModifiedBy should be a trusted management process or SYSTEM — user-mode processes modifying rules are suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Software updates modifying their own pre-existing firewall rules
Group Policy applying updated rule configurations from a new baseline
Administrative tools adjusting rules during maintenance windows

keyFields.name: RuleName
keyFields.description: Name of the modified firewall rule

keyFields.name: RuleId
keyFields.description: Unique identifier for the rule; compare with EID 2004 to see before/after state

keyFields.name: ApplicationPath
keyFields.description: Binary path associated with the rule

keyFields.name: ModifiedBy
keyFields.description: Process or policy source that modified the rule

entries.title: Firewall rule deleted
entries.summary: A firewall rule was removed from the exception list.
entries.details: Generated when a Windows Defender Firewall rule is deleted. Records the rule name, ID, direction, and the process or policy source responsible for the deletion.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

investigationPivots: Identify whether the deleted rule was an allow or block rule — deletion of block rules is higher severity
Deletion of rules for services on ports 3389, 445, or 135 warrants immediate escalation
Correlate ModifiedBy with process creation events to identify the responsible binary
Check if EID 2004 shows a matching RuleId was added shortly before deletion (indicates attacker cleanup)
commonFalsePositives: Software uninstallers removing their own registered firewall rules
IT policy cleanup removing stale or expired rules
Group Policy removing rules superseded by a newer baseline

keyFields.name: RuleName
keyFields.description: Name of the deleted firewall rule

keyFields.name: RuleId
keyFields.description: Unique identifier of the deleted rule

keyFields.name: Direction
keyFields.description: Whether the deleted rule applied to inbound or outbound traffic

keyFields.name: ModifiedBy
keyFields.description: Process or policy source that deleted the rule

entries.title: Firewall rules failed to load
entries.summary: Windows Defender Firewall could not load rules from the local policy store.
entries.details: Generated when the Windows Firewall service fails to load rules from the local Group Policy store or registry, leaving the system operating without expected policy constraints. This may follow GPO corruption, registry manipulation, or malicious tampering with firewall policy storage (typically under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy). While often a benign configuration error, this event occurring in proximity to other firewall or defense-evasion events should be treated as potential policy tamper until ruled out.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

investigationPivots: Inspect HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SharedAccess\Parameters\FirewallPolicy for unexpected modifications
Review System log for GPO processing errors (EID 1085) around the same timestamp
Verify the effective firewall policy and running service state after this event fires
commonFalsePositives: Policy store corruption after an abrupt shutdown or failed OS update
First-boot configuration issues in newly provisioned or cloned environments

entries.title: All firewall rules deleted
entries.summary: All rules in the Windows Defender Firewall exception list were deleted.
entries.details: Generated when all rules are removed from the Windows Defender Firewall exception list for a profile. Bulk rule deletion effectively removes all traffic filtering for the affected profile. This can be achieved via netsh advfirewall reset, PowerShell Remove-NetFirewallRule -All, or direct registry manipulation.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

investigationPivots: Correlate with process creation logs to identify the process that invoked the bulk deletion
Look for netsh.exe, powershell.exe, or wmic.exe in EID 4688 or Sysmon EID 1 near the event timestamp
Review network connection events immediately after — an open firewall is often followed by C2 or lateral movement activity
Confirm whether this was authorized via a change management record
commonFalsePositives: Authorized firewall policy reset during system reimaging or reprovisioning
Enterprise security tools performing a controlled policy reset before applying a new baseline

entries.title: Windows Firewall active profile changed
entries.summary: The active Windows Defender Firewall network profile has switched.
entries.details: Generated when the active firewall profile switches between Domain, Private, and Public. The active profile determines which rule set is enforced, and profiles typically differ significantly in their permissiveness. Adversaries may exploit profile switching to bypass stricter Domain profile rules — for example, by disconnecting the endpoint from the domain network to force a switch to the Public or Private profile, where rules may permit broader inbound access. Unexpected Domain-to-Public switches on domain-joined endpoints are anomalous and should be correlated with network adapter or DHCP events.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify System Firewall

investigationPivots: Determine if the new profile (NewProfile) is less restrictive than the previous profile
Correlate with network adapter and DHCP events in the System log to understand the trigger
On domain-joined endpoints, a switch away from Domain profile during active business hours is anomalous
commonFalsePositives: Laptops switching between office (Domain) and home/remote (Private) networks
VPN connections and disconnections triggering profile changes
Virtual machines with dynamic or bridged network adapters
